title: Define a Modeled Component

Define a Modeled Component

Define a Modeled Component

Defining modeled components is the same as defining normal components. The only difference is that you can define generic entities and fields, and you can write ProcScript that references those generic objects.

You must have created a project to contain your application definitions. For more information, see Working with Projects.

Modeled components are specific to the type of component, thus a service modeled component can only be used for services, a DSP modeled component only for DSPs. You cannot change the component type.

To define a modeled component:

  1. Create a modeled component in a project.
    1. Open the project to which the modeled component will belong.
    2. In the Resource Browser, select the template for the type of modeled component you want to create, for example Modeled SVC Component and drag and drop it on the project in the Project Structure Editor.
    3. Rename the modeled component.
    4. Double-click the modeled component open it for editing. Notice that the Is Modeled Component property of the modeled component is set to TrueFor a normal component or a derived component, it is False.
  2. Edit the properties and script of the component as required.
  3. Define the component structure. For services and server pages, use the Define Structure worksheet. For forms and reports, use the Define Frames worksheet. For more information, see Define the Structure and Layout of Forms and Reports and Define the Component Structure.

    To create entities and fields, use the Resource Browser to select templates or modeled objects and add them to the structure.

    1. To define derived entities and fields, select the modeled entity or field in the Model tab.
    2. To define other entities and fields, select the object in the Templates tab.
    3. To make an entity or field generic, set its Is Generic property to True.

      For more information, see Generic and Bound Entities and Fields.

    Note: If you changed the component structure of a modeled component after it has been used to create derived components, the changed structure is not inherited by the derived component. Neither are changes made to properties, constants, and ProcScript of the entities and fields.
  4. Edit the properties and script of the entities and fields, as required. For information on defining specific types of modeled components, such as for a server page or a service, read the documentation about that component type. For more information, see Script the Component Behavior.
    Tip: When referencing constants and generic entities and fields in ProcScript, ensure that you enclose them in angle brackets; for example <MYENT.GENERIC>. This generalizes the code and enables Uniface to automatically substitute the real names of entities and fields in the derived component.
  5. If applicable, define the component layout, just as you would for a normal component. For more information, see Create the Component Layout of Server Pages and Define the Structure and Layout of Forms and Reports.
  6. Optionally, compile the modeled component to check the syntax of ProcScript.
Related Links
  • Component Data Structure

    Every component has a data structure that defines its component entities and fields, and the path required to access the data. The data structure affects the validation rules for data I/O, such as enforcing referential integrity constraints.

  • Generic and Bound Entities and Fields

    A modeled component usually contains generic entities and fields that act as placeholders for entities and fields in the derived component.
